content_markdown:
Satellite X is in orbit around the Earth. An identical satellite Y is in a higher orbit. What is correct for the total energy and the kinetic energy of the satellite Y compared with satellite X?

| | Total energy of satellite Y | Kinetic energy of satellite Y |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| A. | larger | larger |
| B. | smaller | larger |
| C. | larger | smaller |
| D. | smaller | smaller |

diagram_metadata.description: A table showing four options (A, B, C, D) comparing the total energy and kinetic energy of satellite Y relative to satellite X.

options.label: A
options.text: Total energy: larger; Kinetic energy: larger

options.label: B
options.text: Total energy: smaller; Kinetic energy: larger

options.label: C
options.text: Total energy: larger; Kinetic energy: smaller

options.label: D
options.text: Total energy: smaller; Kinetic energy: smaller

vibe_explanation: For a satellite in a circular orbit, the total energy is $E_{total} = -\frac{GMm}{2r}$ and the kinetic energy is $E_k = \frac{GMm}{2r}$. As the orbital radius $r$ increases for satellite Y, the total energy becomes less negative (meaning it increases/is larger), while the kinetic energy decreases (meaning it is smaller) because the orbital speed $v = \sqrt{\frac{GM}{r}}$ is lower at higher altitudes.
